sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 13 and Ch. 14 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. From Ch. 13 (I-O psychology) only the conceptual items were kept; US-employment-law and history-trivia items (Q1, Q5, Q7) were excluded as off-scope for MCAT PSBB. Live OpenStax pages show CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: Job satisfaction, the thing this question asks about, is best defined as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An employee's overall attitude or feelings about their job

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total salary an employee earns

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of hours an employee works per week

choices.label: D
choices.text: The physical safety of the workplace

hints.rationale: Job satisfaction is the attitudinal/emotional evaluation an employee has about their job.

hints.prompt: Human factors psychology is chiefly concerned with which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The fit between workers and their tools, equipment, and physical environment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Employees' emotional attitudes toward their jobs

hints.rationale: Human factors psychology focuses on the person-equipment/environment fit, not on workers' attitudes.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a recognized branch of industrial-organizational psychology?
hints.rationale: Advertising psychology is not a branch of I-O psychology, so that option can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which area of I-O psychology measures job satisfaction?
questions.explanation: Organizational psychology, the branch of I-O psychology concerned with workers' feelings and relationships, is the area that measures job satisfaction.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 13 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: What core assumption do Theory X managers make about employees?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Employees dislike work and must be closely controlled and monitored

choices.label: B
choices.text: Employees are inherently motivated and seek responsibility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Employees value autonomy above all else

choices.label: D
choices.text: Employees perform best with no supervision at all

hints.rationale: Theory X assumes workers are unmotivated and require close, distrustful control.

hints.prompt: How does Theory Y differ from Theory X?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Theory Y assumes employees are self-motivated and thrive with autonomy and trust

choices.label: B
choices.text: Theory Y assumes employees need constant surveillance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Theory Y and Theory X make identical assumptions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Theory Y is only about designing machines

hints.rationale: Theory Y managers assume employees are self-directed and grant autonomy, the opposite of Theory X.

hints.prompt: Which pair of workplace practices grants employees flexibility and autonomy (hallmarks of Theory Y, not Theory X)?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Monitored bathroom breaks and mandatory time clocks

hints.rationale: Telecommuting and flextime are autonomy-granting (Theory Y) practices and can be eliminated as Theory X examples.

questions.stem: Which practice is an example of Theory X management?
questions.explanation: Theory X managers assume employees are unmotivated and need close control; keystroke monitoring reflects that distrustful, surveillance-based approach.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 13 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: In I-O psychology, 'job satisfaction' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An employee's overall positive or negative evaluation of their job

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of employees in a company

choices.label: D
choices.text: The length of the standard workday

hints.rationale: Job satisfaction is the employee's overall attitude toward the job.

hints.prompt: In studies of job satisfaction, the factor called 'work content' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The nature of the work itself and the tasks performed

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of vacation days offered

hints.rationale: 'Work content' means the nature of the work and tasks themselves.

hints.prompt: Research on job satisfaction generally finds that financial rewards (pay) are which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only weakly related to overall job satisfaction compared with other factors

choices.label: B
choices.text: The single strongest predictor of overall job satisfaction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Completely unrelated to satisfaction in every study

choices.label: D
choices.text: The only factor that determines satisfaction

hints.rationale: Pay is only weakly predictive of overall satisfaction, so 'financial rewards' can be eliminated as the strongest factor.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most strongly predictive factor of overall job satisfaction?
questions.explanation: Per the source, work content (the nature of the work itself) is the most strongly predictive factor of overall job satisfaction.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 13 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: Human factors psychology is primarily concerned with which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The fit between people and the physical tools, equipment, and environment they use

hints.rationale: Human factors psychology studies the fit between people and their physical work environment/equipment.

hints.prompt: Which workstation concern would fall under organizational psychology rather than human factors psychology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An employee's relationships and interactions with a supervisor and coworkers

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ergonomic design of a keyboard

choices.label: C
choices.text: The height of a desk chair

hints.rationale: Interpersonal/relationship concerns fall under organizational psychology, not human factors.

hints.prompt: Which pair of workstation issues is about social/interpersonal dynamics rather than physical ergonomics?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Closeness to the supervisor and frequency of coworker visits

hints.rationale: Closeness to a supervisor and coworker visits are interpersonal, not ergonomic, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: What aspect of an office workstation would a human factors psychologist be concerned about?
questions.explanation: Human factors psychology focuses on the fit between people and their physical work environment, such as the ergonomic height of a chair.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 13 Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: In Lazarus's appraisal model of stress, a 'primary appraisal' of an event involves judging whether it is which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Threatening, harmful, or challenging to one's well-being

hints.rationale: Primary appraisal evaluates whether an event is threatening, harmful, or challenging.

hints.prompt: How does appraising an event as a 'challenge' differ from appraising it as a 'threat'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A challenge appraisal sees potential for growth or mastery, while a threat appraisal anticipates harm or loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are two names for exactly the same appraisal

choices.label: C
choices.text: A challenge appraisal always causes more harm than a threat appraisal

choices.label: D
choices.text: A threat appraisal is always experienced as positive

hints.rationale: Challenge appraisals are growth-oriented, whereas threat appraisals anticipate harm.

hints.prompt: Which appraisal is LEAST likely to produce strong negative stress effects for a person?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Viewing an event as challenging, or as mainly affecting other people rather than oneself

choices.label: B
choices.text: Viewing an event as a direct personal threat

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viewing an event as personally catastrophic

hints.rationale: Appraising an event as a challenge, or as not personally threatening, produces less harm, eliminating those options.

questions.stem: Negative effects of stress are most likely to be experienced when an event is perceived as ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: negative, but it is likely to affect one's friends rather than oneself

choices.label: D
choices.text: threatening, and no clear options for dealing with it are apparent

questions.explanation: Stress is most harmful when an event is appraised as threatening and the person sees no clear way to cope with it (a primary appraisal of threat without perceived control).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: Selye's general adaptation syndrome (GAS) describes which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The body's staged physiological response to prolonged stress

hints.rationale: GAS is Selye's model of the body's staged response to stress.

hints.prompt: What is the correct order of the three stages of the general adaptation syndrome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alarm reaction, then resistance, then exhaustion

hints.rationale: GAS proceeds from alarm to resistance to exhaustion.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is NOT one of the three named stages of the general adaptation syndrome?
hints.rationale: 'Fight-or-flight' is part of the alarm reaction, not a separate named GAS stage, so it can be eliminated.

questions.stem: At which stage of Selye's general adaptation syndrome is a person especially vulnerable to illness?
questions.explanation: In Selye's general adaptation syndrome, the exhaustion stage follows prolonged stress and depletes the body's resources, leaving the person especially vulnerable to illness.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q3 = A.

hints.prompt: The Holmes and Rahe Social Readjustment Rating Scale (SRRS) measures which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The amount of life change or readjustment associated with various life events

hints.rationale: The SRRS assigns 'life change units' quantifying the readjustment an event requires.

hints.prompt: On the SRRS, how are life events ranked?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The more disruptive a life event, the higher its life-change-unit score

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only negative events are counted at all

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every event is assigned exactly the same score

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pleasant events always receive the highest scores

hints.rationale: Higher SRRS scores reflect greater required readjustment.

hints.prompt: The single highest-ranked SRRS item is a major, permanent life loss. Which two listed events are more recoverable and thus rank BELOW the top item?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The permanent loss of one's closest lifelong relationship

hints.rationale: Marriage and personal illness rank lower than the most disruptive relationship loss, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: According to the Holmes and Rahe scale, which life event requires the greatest amount of readjustment?
questions.explanation: On the Holmes and Rahe Social Readjustment Rating Scale, death of a spouse is the highest-ranked life event, requiring the greatest readjustment.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q5 = D.

hints.prompt: The concept of 'cognitive appraisal' in stress research refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How an individual personally interprets and evaluates an event

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of items printed on a questionnaire

choices.label: C
choices.text: The geographic region where participants live

hints.rationale: Appraisal is the individual's subjective interpretation of an event.

hints.prompt: Why might the same life event (for example, a divorce) be far more stressful for one person than for another?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because people appraise and interpret the same event differently

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the event has a fixed, identical impact on everyone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because stress is entirely genetic and unchangeable

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because rating scales always measure stress perfectly

hints.rationale: Individual appraisal makes identical events differ in impact, a limitation if a scale assigns each event a fixed value.

hints.prompt: Two of the main-question options criticize the scale's SAMPLE or LENGTH rather than how it conceptualizes stress. A student focused on the stress-appraisal process would set aside which two?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'It has too few items' and 'it was developed using only people from one region'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'It ignores how a person appraises an event' and 'it assigns fixed values'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'It treats stress as subjective' and 'it uses life-change units'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'It overlooks interpretation' and 'it ignores individual differences'

hints.rationale: 'Too few items' and 'limited regional sample' concern length and sample, not the stress-appraisal process, so they can be set aside.

questions.stem: What is one of the major criticisms of the Social Readjustment Rating Scale?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It was developed using only people from the New England region of the United States.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It does not take into consideration how a person appraises an event.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the items included are positive.

questions.explanation: A major criticism of the Social Readjustment Rating Scale is that it assigns fixed values to events and ignores how the individual appraises (interprets) each event.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: What is the general role of the immune system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To identify and destroy foreign invaders such as pathogens

choices.label: B
choices.text: To pump blood throughout the body

choices.label: C
choices.text: To digest food in the stomach

choices.label: D
choices.text: To transmit nerve signals to muscles

hints.rationale: The immune system defends the body against foreign invaders and pathogens.

hints.prompt: White blood cells are living cells, whereas antibodies are which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proteins produced to target specific invaders (not cells themselves)

choices.label: B
choices.text: A specific type of white blood cell

hints.rationale: Antibodies are proteins, not white blood cells, distinguishing them from the cells that attack invaders.

hints.prompt: Which option is NOT part of the immune defense at all, but rather a protective cap on the ends of chromosomes?
hints.rationale: Telomeres are chromosome caps unrelated to attacking invaders, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The white blood cells that attack foreign invaders to the body are called ________.
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes are the white blood cells of the immune system that identify and attack foreign invaders such as pathogens.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: The Type A behavior pattern is characterized by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Competitiveness, time urgency, impatience, and hostility

hints.rationale: Type A is marked by competitiveness, time urgency, impatience, and hostility.

hints.prompt: The Type A behavior pattern has been linked most strongly to which health outcome?
hints.rationale: The Type A pattern is linked to coronary heart disease.

hints.prompt: Type A traits can be grouped into an achievement/pace cluster and an interpersonal/emotional cluster. Which two traits belong to the achievement/pace cluster?
hints.rationale: Time urgency and competitive drive form the achievement/pace cluster and can be set aside from the emotional traits.

questions.stem: The most lethal dimension of Type A behavior pattern seems to be ________.
questions.explanation: Research indicates that hostility is the component of the Type A behavior pattern most strongly linked to coronary heart disease.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: Emotion-focused coping is best described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Managing one's emotional response to a stressor rather than changing the stressor itself

choices.label: B
choices.text: Directly solving the problem causing the stress

hints.rationale: Emotion-focused coping regulates emotional reactions rather than altering the stressor.

hints.prompt: Problem-focused coping is most useful for stressors that are ________, whereas emotion-focused coping suits stressors that are ________.

choices.label: D
choices.text: minor; exactly the same as minor

hints.rationale: Problem-focused coping fits controllable stressors; emotion-focused coping fits uncontrollable ones.

hints.prompt: Which two of the listed stressors are generally CONTROLLABLE through direct action (and thus better suited to problem-focused coping)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Poor grades in school and unemployment

choices.label: C
choices.text: An incurable disease and a permanent loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: An irreversible medical diagnosis and grief

hints.rationale: Poor grades and unemployment can be addressed with direct action, so they suit problem-focused coping and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Emotion-focused coping would likely be a better method than problem-focused coping for dealing with which of the following stressors?
questions.explanation: Emotion-focused coping is better suited to uncontrollable stressors. A terminal illness cannot be changed by action, so managing the emotional response is more adaptive.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: In health psychology, 'social support' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The comfort, care, and help available from other people

choices.label: B
choices.text: The amount of money a person earns

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of hours a person sleeps

hints.rationale: Social support is the help and comfort available from one's social network.

hints.prompt: The 'stress-buffering' role of social support means that support does which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protects health by reducing the harmful impact of stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increases the harmful impact of stress

choices.label: C
choices.text: Has no measurable relationship to stress

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directly causes disease on its own

hints.rationale: Social support buffers and reduces the harmful effects of stress.

hints.prompt: If social support protects health, then well-supported people should show which pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fewer stress-related health problems, not more

choices.label: B
choices.text: More asthma and more heart disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Worse overall health than unsupported people

choices.label: D
choices.text: Higher rates of essentially every illness

hints.rationale: Protective support predicts fewer, not more, stress-related illnesses, eliminating the 'more disease' options.

questions.stem: Relative to those with low levels of social support, individuals with high levels of social support ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: are more likely to develop asthma

choices.label: B
choices.text: tend to have less perceived control

choices.label: C
choices.text: are more likely to develop cardiovascular disorders

questions.explanation: High levels of social support buffer the effects of stress, so well-supported individuals tend to tolerate stress better and have better health outcomes.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: In positive psychology, the three presumed components of happiness are the pleasant life, the good life, and the meaningful life. These refer to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pleasurable experiences, using one's talents, and using talents to help others

hints.rationale: The pleasant, good, and meaningful life correspond to pleasure, using one's talents, and helping others.

hints.prompt: The 'meaningful life' component of happiness specifically involves which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using one's talents in the service of others or something larger than oneself

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acquiring as much material wealth as possible

hints.rationale: The meaningful life involves using one's talents to serve others or a greater good.

hints.prompt: Two of the main-question options describe USING or APPLYING one's talents (the good life and the meaningful life). Which pair is that?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Using our talents to help improve the lives of others' and 'identifying and using our talents to enrich our lives'

hints.rationale: Those two options map onto the good life and the meaningful life, so they are genuine components and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not one of the presumed components of happiness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: using our talents to help improve the lives of others

choices.label: D
choices.text: identifying and using our talents to enrich our lives

questions.explanation: The presumed components of happiness are the pleasant life (pleasurable experiences), the good life (using one's talents), and the meaningful life (using talents to help others). 'Learning new skills' is not one of these components.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q17 = B.

hints.prompt: In psychology, 'positive affect' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The experience of pleasant emotions and moods

choices.label: B
choices.text: An expectation that the future will turn out well

hints.rationale: Positive affect is the experience of pleasant feeling states (emotions and moods).

hints.prompt: 'Optimism' is best defined as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A general expectation that good things will happen in the future

choices.label: C
choices.text: A measure of a person's intelligence

hints.rationale: Optimism is a cognitive expectation that good things will happen in the future.

hints.prompt: Two main-question options claim one construct is 'more scientific' than the other. Is that framing relevant to the actual difference between the two constructs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, 'which is more scientific' does not describe the real difference between feelings and expectations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, optimism is measurably more scientific than positive affect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, positive affect is measurably more scientific than optimism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, science determines which feelings a person has

hints.rationale: The 'more scientific' framing is a distractor and does not capture the feelings-versus-expectations distinction, eliminating those two options.

questions.stem: How does positive affect differ from optimism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Optimism is more scientific than positive affect.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive affect is more scientific than optimism.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive affect involves feeling states, whereas optimism involves expectations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Optimism involves feeling states, whereas positive affect involves expectations.

questions.explanation: Positive affect refers to pleasant feeling states (emotions and moods), whereas optimism is a cognitive expectation that good things will happen in the future.
